sources.notes: A character alternates walking and resting under a health budget; find the minimum time to cover distance d.
Official editorial checks a proposed number of rests with a triangular-cost formula and binary-searches the answer.

annotation.summary: Find the minimum time to walk distance d when consecutive walking steps have increasing health cost and resting recovers health.
annotation.constraints: h and d are large, so simulating every step is impossible; only the number and length of walking blocks should be considered.
annotation.core_idea: If there are r rests, the walk is split into r+1 blocks. For fixed r, the optimal distribution of walking steps is as balanced as possible because the cost of a block of length x is triangular, x(x+1)/2, a convex function. This gives a monotone feasibility test for whether r rests can cover d under health h, so binary search the minimum r and return d+r time.
annotation.complexity: O(log d * log d) or O(log d) with closed-form block length computation.
tricks: monotone feasibility in number of rests

solution_variants.name: binary-search-rests
solution_variants.summary: Binary search the number of rests; for each value, compute the maximum distance coverable by optimally balanced walking blocks.

solution_variants.name: ternary-search-block-length
solution_variants.summary: Equivalent approach optimizing over the common block length using the convexity of triangular costs.

tags.evidence: The official solution binary-searches the minimum number of rests using a monotone feasibility check.
If a certain number of rests is enough, any larger number of rests is also enough.
The walking cost per block is a triangular-number sum and the optimum uses balanced block sizes.
Ternary search is an alternative way to optimize the convex block-length expression, but binary search on rests is the main reviewed solution.
